questions.question: What's the difference between thenApply and thenCompose on a CompletableFuture?
choices: Identical; thenApply is a deprecated alias for thenCompose.

thenApply takes a synchronous Function<T,R> and produces CompletableFuture<R>. thenCompose takes a Function<T, CompletableFuture<R>> and flattens the nested future, so chaining further async work doesn't produce CompletableFuture<CompletableFuture<R>>.
thenApply runs on the common pool; thenCompose blocks until done.
thenApply requires an executor; thenCompose does not.
questions.explanation: thenApply is the sync-transform combinator (like .NET's ContinueWith returning a value). thenCompose is the flatMap analogue: when the next step is itself async, thenCompose unwraps the nested future so the result type stays CompletableFuture<R>.

questions.question: Why is Executors.newCachedThreadPool() risky for a service with unknown or bursty load?
choices: It only supports 8 threads at most.
It creates threads on demand without an upper bound; a sudden burst can spawn thousands of threads, exhausting memory and degrading throughput.
It uses single-thread execution under the hood.
questions.explanation: newCachedThreadPool has no max-pool-size bound. Under burst load it spawns one thread per submitted task, which can consume large amounts of memory (each thread is ~1MB of stack on default settings) and cause GC/CPU contention. Prefer a sized pool whose limit you've reasoned about.

questions.question: What is the closest analogue in Java 17 to C#'s 'await someAsyncCall()' inside a method?
choices: An await keyword exists in Java 17 since LTS.

There is no language-level await. You compose explicitly: someAsyncCall().thenApply(...) or thenCompose(...). Virtual threads in Java 21 reduce the need for this style by making blocking I/O cheap on lightweight threads.
The synchronized keyword waits for an async result.
questions.explanation: Java 17 has no await. CompletableFuture composition (thenApply, thenCompose, thenCombine, exceptionally) is the explicit alternative. Java 21's virtual threads let you write blocking code that scales without the await-style rewrite - a fundamentally different solution path.
